questions.question: The magnetic field at the centre of a circular current-carrying loop is:
options: Parallel to the plane of the loop
Perpendicular to the plane of the loop
questions.explanation: The field at the centre of a circular loop is straight and perpendicular to the plane of the loop. Each segment of the loop contributes to a net field directed along the axis.

questions.question: The strength of the magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop increases when:
questions.explanation: Field strength at the centre is directly proportional to the current. More current produces a stronger field. It is also proportional to the number of turns and inversely proportional to the radius.

questions.question: What does the magnetic field pattern of a current-carrying solenoid resemble?
questions.explanation: The field pattern of a solenoid — uniform inside, emerging from one end and entering the other — closely resembles that of a bar magnet.

questions.question: Which rule helps determine the polarity of a solenoid?
questions.explanation: Using the clock rule: if you look at one end of the solenoid and the current flows anticlockwise, that end is a North pole. Clockwise current indicates a South pole. The right-hand grip rule can also be applied.

questions.question: Inside an ideal current-carrying solenoid, the magnetic field is:
questions.explanation: The field inside a solenoid is strong and uniform, with parallel field lines directed along the axis. Outside the solenoid, the field is weak and similar to a bar magnet's external field.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT increase the magnetic field strength of a solenoid?
options: Increasing the length while keeping turns constant
questions.explanation: Increasing the length while keeping the number of turns constant spreads the turns farther apart, reducing turns per unit length. This decreases the field strength. All other options increase it.

questions.question: An electromagnet consists of:
options: A permanent magnet with a coil around it
A solenoid with a soft iron core
A solenoid with a steel core
A straight wire wrapped in insulation
questions.explanation: An electromagnet is made by placing a soft iron core inside a solenoid. Soft iron greatly increases the field strength and loses magnetism when current stops — ideal for on/off control.

questions.question: The North pole of a solenoid is the end where the current, when viewed from that end, flows:
questions.explanation: Using the clock rule: if current flows anticlockwise when viewed from one end, that end behaves as a North pole. Clockwise current produces a South pole.

questions.question: Which of the following is a practical use of an electromagnet?
questions.explanation: Electric bells use electromagnets to repeatedly attract and release an armature, producing the ringing sound. Compasses and fridge magnets use permanent magnets. Lodestone is a natural magnet.

questions.question: If the current through a solenoid is reversed, what happens to its polarity?
questions.explanation: Reversing the current reverses the direction of the magnetic field, so the North and South poles of the solenoid swap ends.

questions.question: Which factor distinguishes an electromagnet from a permanent magnet?
options: Electromagnets can be switched on and off
questions.explanation: The key advantage of an electromagnet is that its magnetism can be controlled — switched on or off — by controlling the current. Permanent magnets cannot be turned off.

questions.question: The magnetic field outside a solenoid is:
options: Weak and similar to a bar magnet's external field
questions.explanation: Outside a solenoid, the field lines spread out and the field is much weaker than inside. The pattern is similar to the external field of a bar magnet.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered circular loops, solenoids, and electromagnets!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the clock rule for solenoid polarity and factors affecting field strength.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the field pattern of solenoids, electromagnet construction, and the difference between electromagnets and permanent magnets.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: circular loop field direction, solenoid as a bar magnet analogue, clock rule, and uses of electromagnets.
